reasoning: The Lean statement faithfully encodes the informal problem. `Finset.Icc 1 n` gives {1,...,n}; `boxes : Finset.Icc 1 n → Fin k` encodes a partition into k labeled boxes; the filtered sum `∑ x ∈ Finset.univ.filter (boxes · = i), (x : ℕ)` gives the sum of the values in box i; and `∀ i j, sum i = sum j` says all boxes have equal sums. Although the Lean encoding allows empty boxes syntactically, since all elements of {1,...,n} are positive, any empty box would have sum 0 and force all boxes (including non-empty ones) to have sum 0, which is impossible — so the equal-sum constraint automatically forces every box to be non-empty, matching the informal interpretation. `IsGreatest kboxes (⌈n/2⌉)` correctly asserts that ⌈n/2⌉ is both achievable and an upper bound, capturing "largest k". The hypothesis `n > 0` matches "positive integer n". The answer ⌈n/2⌉ matches the known Putnam 2010 A1 solution.
